title: Merge two sorted arrays
description: Merge two sorted arrays into a single sorted array

examples.explanation: Step 1: Create combined array of size 6. Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [1, 3, 5, _, _, _]. Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6]. Step 4: Sort combined → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].
Step 1: Create combined array of size 5. Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [10, 20, _, _, _]. Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [10, 20, 5, 15, 25]. Step 4: Sort combined → [5, 10, 15, 20, 25].

approaches.name: Brute Force O((m+n) log(m+n))
approaches.summary: Concatenate both arrays into a new array and sort it using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. This is straightforward but inefficient as it ignores the existing sorted order of the input arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Two Sorted Arrays Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution: concatenate two sorted arrays and sort the result
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to merging two sorted arrays ignores their existing sorted structure entirely. The intuition is simple: if we combine all elements from both arrays into one collection, then sort that collection, we'll get a sorted merged result. This works because sorting any collection of numbers will arrange them in ascending order, regardless of their original arrangement. The algorithm has three clear steps: 1) Create a new array with enough capacity to hold all elements from both input arrays. 2) Copy all elements from the first array into this new array. 3) Copy all elements from the second array into the remaining positions. 4) Apply a standard sorting algorithm (like quicksort or mergesort) to the combined array. The time complexity is O((m+n) log(m+n)) where m and n are the lengths of the input arrays, because we're sorting m+n elements. The space complexity is O(m+n) since we create a new array of that size. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, working solution, though inefficient. Common mistakes include forgetting to allocate enough space for the combined array, incorrectly calculating indices when copying, or using an unstable sort when stability matters. The key insight is that while this approach works, it's suboptimal because it doesn't leverage the fact that both input arrays are already sorted.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create combined array of size 6. Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [1, 3, 5, _, _, _]. Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6]. Step 4: Sort combined → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].
Step 1: Create combined array of size 5. Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [10, 20, _, _, _]. Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [10, 20, 5, 15, 25]. Step 4: Sort combined → [5, 10, 15, 20, 25].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting a Combined Array
ladders.desc: Sort an unsorted array using a standard algorithm
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: taking an unsorted array and arranging its elements in ascending order. The intuition is that by comparing and rearranging elements, we can transform any random order into sorted order. We'll use quicksort as our example algorithm, which works on the divide-and-conquer principle: 1) Choose a pivot element from the array. 2) Partition the array so all elements less than the pivot come before it, and all greater come after. 3) Recursively apply the same process to the left and right partitions. The time complexity is O(n log n) on average for n elements, with O(n²) worst-case if poor pivots are chosen. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the final step after concatenation. Common mistakes include not handling base cases in recursion, choosing bad pivots (like always using the first element), or incorrect partition logic. The key insight is that sorting is a well-studied problem with efficient algorithms, though here we're applying it to already-partially-sorted data.

examples.trace: Step 1: Choose pivot = 2. Step 2: Partition → left = [1], right = [3], pivot = [2]. Step 3: Sort left (already sorted). Step 4: Sort right (already sorted). Step 5: Combine → [1, 2, 3].

Step 1: Choose pivot = 3. Step 2: Partition → left = [2, 1], right = [5, 4], pivot = [3]. Step 3: Sort left: pivot=1 → left=[], right=[2], combine → [1, 2]. Step 4: Sort right: pivot=4 → left=[], right=[5], combine → [4, 5]. Step 5: Combine all → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Concatenating Two Arrays
ladders.desc: Combine two arrays into a single larger array
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is concatenation: creating a new array that contains all elements from both input arrays in their original order. The intuition is that by simply putting all elements together, we create a collection that can then be sorted. The algorithm is straightforward: 1) Calculate the total size as length(arr1) + length(arr2). 2) Create a new array of that size. 3) Copy all elements from arr1 into the beginning of the new array. 4) Copy all elements from arr2 into the remaining positions. Time complexity is O(m+n) since we iterate through both arrays once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the new array. This connects to L1 by providing the unsorted array that needs sorting. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, not allocating enough space, or modifying the original arrays unintentionally. The key insight is that concatenation preserves the original sequences but doesn't maintain overall sortedness unless the arrays were already in relation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Total size = 2 + 2 = 4. Step 2: Create new array of size 4. Step 3: Copy arr1 → positions 0-1: [1, 2, _, _]. Step 4: Copy arr2 → positions 2-3: [1, 2, 3, 4].
Step 1: Total size = 1 + 2 = 3. Step 2: Create new array of size 3. Step 3: Copy arr1 → position 0: [10, _, _]. Step 4: Copy arr2 → positions 1-2: [10, 20, 30].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Copying with Indices
ladders.desc: Copy elements from one array to another at specific positions
ladders.explanation: This building block involves copying a sequence of elements from a source array to a destination array, starting at a given index. The intuition is that arrays are contiguous blocks of memory, and we can transfer elements by iterating through positions. The algorithm: 1) Start at the given destination index. 2) For each element in the source array, assign it to the destination array at the current index. 3) Increment the index after each assignment. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements copied. Space complexity is O(1) additional space (not counting the arrays themselves). This connects to L2 as the mechanism for concatenation. Common mistakes include incorrect index arithmetic, copying beyond array bounds, or using shallow copies for objects. The key insight is that array copying is a fundamental operation that requires careful index management.

examples.trace: Step 1: dest[1] = src[0] = 1. Step 2: dest[2] = src[1] = 2. Step 3: dest[3] = src[2] = 3. Result: dest indices 1-3 now [1, 2, 3].
Step 1: dest[2] = src[0] = 10. Step 2: dest[3] = src[1] = 20. Result: dest indices 2-3 now [10, 20].

testCases.input: src = [a, b], dest = [x, y, z], startIdx = 0

testCases.expected: dest = [50, 60, 70] (no change, startIdx out of bounds)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Assigning Array Elements
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental actions: accessing an element at a given index (reading its value) and assigning a value to an element at a given index (writing). The intuition is that arrays provide direct, random access to their elements via indices, which are typically zero-based. Accessing: given an array arr and index i, retrieve arr[i]. Assigning: given array arr, index i, and value v, set arr[i] = v. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(1) additional. This connects to L3 as the atomic actions used during copying. Common mistakes include using indices outside the array bounds (causing errors), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, or misunderstanding that assignment modifies the array. The key insight is that arrays are indexed collections where each position holds a value.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check index 1 is within bounds (0 to 2). Step 2: Retrieve value at position 1: arr[1] = 20.
Step 1: Check index 0 is within bounds (0 to 2). Step 2: Set arr[0] = 100. Result: arr now [100, 6, 7].

testCases.input: Assign: arr = [a, b], index = 0, value = c
Assign: arr = [x, y, z], index = 2, value = w

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Ordered Lists
ladders.desc: Basic concept: an array is an ordered collection of elements accessible by position
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as fundamental data structures. An array is an ordered list of elements, where each element has a specific position called an index (usually starting at 0). The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes numbered consecutively, each holding one value. Key properties: 1) Fixed size (typically) when created. 2) Elements are stored contiguously in memory. 3) Direct access to any element via its index in O(1) time. 4) All elements are of the same type in typed languages. For example, an array of integers [5, 10, 15] has three elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 10, index 2 = 15. Time complexity for access is O(1); space is O(n) for n elements. This connects to L4 as the structure we operate on. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, confusing arrays with other collections like lists, or not understanding size limitations. The key insight is that arrays provide a simple way to store and retrieve multiple values in order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify array has 3 elements. Step 2: Index 0 holds first element: 3. Step 3: Index 1 holds second element: 7. Step 4: Index 2 holds third element: 1.
Step 1: Array has 2 elements. Step 2: Index 0: 100. Step 3: Index 1: 200.

testCases.expected: Index 0: a, Index 1: b, Index 2: c, Index 3: d

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse both arrays simultaneously, comparing elements and adding the smaller one to a new result array. This efficiently merges the arrays by taking advantage of their sorted order in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Two Sorted Arrays Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete implementation merging two sorted arrays into one sorted array using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach efficiently merges two sorted arrays by taking advantage of their sorted nature. We maintain two pointers, one for each input array, starting at index 0. At each step, we compare the elements at the current pointer positions. The smaller element is appended to the result array, and its pointer is incremented. This process continues until we exhaust one array, after which we append all remaining elements from the other array. The intuition is that since both arrays are sorted, the smallest remaining element overall must be at one of the two pointer positions. By always picking the smaller element, we build the merged array in sorted order. The algorithm runs in O(m+n) time where m and n are array lengths, as each element is processed exactly once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the result array. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle remaining elements after one pointer reaches the end, or incorrectly comparing pointers when arrays have different lengths. This is the complete solution that directly solves the original problem.

examples.trace:
Initialize: i=0 (arr1), j=0 (arr2), result=[]
Step 1: Compare arr1[0]=1 vs arr2[0]=2 → 1 smaller → result=[1], i=1
Step 2: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[0]=2 → 2 smaller → result=[1,2], j=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[1]=4 → 3 smaller → result=[1,2,3], i=2
Step 4: Compare arr1[2]=5 vs arr2[1]=4 → 4 smaller → result=[1,2,3,4], j=2
Step 5: Compare arr1[2]=5 vs arr2[2]=6 → 5 smaller → result=[1,2,3,4,5], i=3
Step 6: i reached end, append remaining arr2[2]=6 → result=[1,2,3,4,5,6]

Initialize: i=0 (arr1), j=0 (arr2), result=[]
Step 1: Compare arr1[0]=10 vs arr2[0]=5 → 5 smaller → result=[5], j=1
Step 2: Compare arr1[0]=10 vs arr2[1]=15 → 10 smaller → result=[5,10], i=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[1]=15 → 15 smaller → result=[5,10,15], j=2
Step 4: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[2]=25 → 20 smaller → result=[5,10,15,20], i=2
Step 5: i reached end, append remaining arr2[2]=25 → result=[5,10,15,20,25]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Subarrays Using Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement the core merging logic that compares elements from two arrays and builds sorted result.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer merging approach. Given two sorted arrays and their current pointer positions, we repeatedly compare the elements at those positions and append the smaller one to a result array. The key insight is that because both arrays are sorted, the smallest remaining element in either array must be at one of the two pointer positions. By always selecting the smaller element, we guarantee the result stays sorted. The algorithm uses a while loop that continues as long as both pointers are within their array bounds. Inside the loop, we compare elements, append the smaller one, and increment that pointer. This sub-routine handles the main merging logic but stops when one array is exhausted. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the minimum of the remaining elements in both arrays. Space complexity is O(k) for the result built so far. This feeds into the full problem by providing the core comparison and selection mechanism. Common mistakes include comparing pointers instead of array elements, or not incrementing the correct pointer after selection. The pseudocode pattern is: while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i += 1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j += 1.

examples.trace:
Start: i=0, j=0, result=[]
Step 1: Compare arr1[0]=1 vs arr2[0]=2 → 1 smaller → result=[1], i=1
Step 2: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[0]=2 → 2 smaller → result=[1,2], j=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[1]=4 → 3 smaller → result=[1,2,3], i=2
Step 4: Compare arr1[2]=5 vs arr2[1]=4 → 4 smaller → result=[1,2,3,4], j=2
Stop: j reached end (j=2, len(arr2)=2)

Start: i=0, j=0, result=[]
Step 1: Compare arr1[0]=10 vs arr2[0]=5 → 5 smaller → result=[5], j=1
Step 2: Compare arr1[0]=10 vs arr2[1]=15 → 10 smaller → result=[5,10], i=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[1]=15 → 15 smaller → result=[5,10,15], j=2
Stop: j reached end (j=2, len(arr2)=2)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare and Select Smaller Element from Two Sources
ladders.desc: Implement the decision logic that compares two elements and selects the smaller one.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the fundamental comparison operation that drives the two-pointer merge. Given two elements from different sources (arrays), we need to determine which one is smaller or equal, and select it for inclusion in the sorted result. The intuition is straightforward: in a sorted merge, we always want the smallest available element. By comparing the current elements from both arrays, we can identify which one should come next. The algorithm is simple: if element1 <= element2, select element1; otherwise, select element2. The equality case (element1 == element2) can go either way, but typically we select from the first array to maintain stability. This logic is executed repeatedly in a loop as part of the larger merge. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the decision mechanism for each iteration. Common mistakes include using strict less-than (<) instead of less-than-or-equal (<=), which can cause incorrect ordering when elements are equal. Another mistake is comparing pointer indices instead of the actual array elements. The pseudocode pattern is: if a <= b: select a else: select b.

examples.trace: Compare 3 and 5 → 3 <= 5 is true → Select 3
Compare 7 and 4 → 7 <= 4 is false → Select 4

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Two Arrays with Separate Pointers
ladders.desc: Simultaneously iterate through two arrays using independent index pointers.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to traverse two arrays concurrently using separate pointer variables. The intuition is that we need to track our position in each array independently because they may progress at different rates. We maintain two pointer variables (typically i and j) that start at 0 and increment independently based on conditions. The key insight is that each pointer only advances when we process an element from its corresponding array. This allows us to compare elements from different positions in the two arrays. The algorithm involves initializing both pointers to 0, then in a loop, we can access arr1[i] and arr2[j]. Based on some condition (like comparing values), we might increment i, j, or both. This pattern is fundamental to many two-pointer algorithms. Time complexity depends on how many iterations we perform. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointers. This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism to access elements for comparison. Common mistakes include incrementing the wrong pointer, or accessing array indices beyond bounds. The pseudocode pattern is: i = 0; j = 0; while condition: # Use arr1[i] and arr2[j]; Update i and/or j.

examples.trace:
Start: i=0, j=0
Step 1: Access arr1[0]=1, arr2[0]=4 → Increment i → i=1, j=0
Step 2: Access arr1[1]=2, arr2[0]=4 → Increment i → i=2, j=0
Step 3: Access arr1[2]=3, arr2[0]=4 → Increment i → i=3, j=0
Stop: i reached end (i=3, len(arr1)=3)

Start: i=0, j=0
Step 1: Access arr1[0]=10, arr2[0]=20 → Increment i → i=1, j=0
Stop: i reached end (i=1, len(arr1)=1)

testCases.input: arr1 = [1, 2], arr2 = [3, 4], steps: increment i twice
arr1 = [5], arr2 = [1, 2, 3], steps: increment j three times
arr1 = [1, 3], arr2 = [2, 4], steps: increment i once, j once
arr1 = [1, 2, 3], arr2 = [], steps: increment i three times
arr1 = [10, 20], arr2 = [5, 15], steps: increment j twice
arr1 = [1], arr2 = [2], steps: increment i once
arr1 = [3, 6], arr2 = [1, 4], steps: increment j once, i once
arr1 = [100], arr2 = [50, 75], steps: increment j once
arr1 = [1, 1, 2], arr2 = [1, 3], steps: increment i twice, j once

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Append Element to Array and Increment Pointer
ladders.desc: Add an element to a result array and advance the corresponding pointer.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation combines two fundamental actions: appending an element to an array and incrementing a pointer variable. The intuition is that when we select an element from one of the input arrays to include in the result, we need to both store it in the result array and move forward in that input array to consider the next element. The algorithm is straightforward: 1) Append the selected element to the result array using something like result.append(element). 2) Increment the pointer corresponding to the array from which the element came (e.g., i += 1 if from arr1). This ensures we don't re-process the same element. Time complexity is O(1) for the append and increment operations (amortized for dynamic arrays). Space complexity is O(1) for the pointer, but O(n) overall for the growing result array. This feeds into the building block by providing the atomic action performed during traversal. Common mistakes include forgetting to increment the pointer (causing infinite loops) or incrementing the wrong pointer. The pseudocode pattern is: result.append(arr[i]); i += 1.

examples.trace:
Current: result=[1,2], arr[2]=3, i=2
Step 1: Append arr[2]=3 to result → result=[1,2,3]
Step 2: Increment i → i=3

Current: result=[], arr[0]=10, i=0
Step 1: Append arr[0]=10 to result → result=[10]
Step 2: Increment i → i=1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index position.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves accessing an element in an array using its index. Arrays are ordered collections where each element has a position starting from 0. The intuition is simple: if you know the position (index) of an element, you can directly retrieve its value. The algorithm is just using array[index] syntax. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20, etc. Time complexity is O(1) for direct index access. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the basic operation by providing the mechanism to get the element to append. Common mistakes include using an index that is out of bounds (e.g., index 3 for an array of length 3, since valid indices are 0,1,2), or confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based counting. Another mistake is trying to access an element before the array is initialized. The pseudocode pattern is: element = arr[i].

examples.trace:
Array arr has elements: index 0 → 5, index 1 → 10, index 2 → 15
Access arr[1] → Returns element at index 1: 10

Array arr has elements: index 0 → 100, index 1 → 200
Access arr[0] → Returns element at index 0: 100

approaches.name: In-Place Merge with Shifts O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Merge the arrays in-place by inserting elements from the second array into the first, shifting elements in the first array as needed. This avoids extra space but is slower due to repeated shifts.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if modifying one input array is allowed, otherwise O(m+n)

ladders.title: Full Problem — In-Place Merge with Shifts O(m*n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution merging two sorted arrays in-place by inserting elements from second array into first with shifting.
ladders.explanation: This approach merges two sorted arrays into a single sorted array by modifying the first array in-place. The intuition is to treat the first array as having extra space (if provided) or to work within its bounds by carefully shifting elements. We iterate through the second array, and for each element, find its correct position in the first array by comparing with elements in the first array. Once found, we shift all elements from that position onward in the first array to the right by one to make space, then insert the element from the second array. This ensures the first array remains sorted throughout the process. The time complexity is O(m*n) because for each of the n elements in the second array, we might shift up to m elements in the worst case (when inserting at the beginning repeatedly). Space complexity is O(1) if we are allowed to modify the first array in-place, as we only use a few extra variables for indices and temporary storage during shifts. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of inserting an element into a sorted array with shifting, which is implemented in L1. Common mistakes include incorrect index management during shifting (leading to overwriting elements), not handling edge cases like empty arrays, and inefficient shifting by shifting one position at a time instead of in bulk. Real code pattern: Initialize i=0 for second array index; while i < len(arr2): find position pos in arr1 for arr2[i] using linear search; shift elements arr1[pos:] right by one; arr1[pos] = arr2[i]; i++. Ensure to update array sizes if they are dynamic.

examples.trace: Start: arr1=[1,3,5,0,0,0], arr2=[2,4,6], m=3, n=3. Process arr2[0]=2: find pos in arr1: compare with 1 (pos0), 3 (pos1) -> insert at pos1. Shift arr1[1:3] right: arr1 becomes [1,3,5,5,0,0] (shifting 5 to index3, 3 to index2? Wait, careful: shift from index1 to end of valid elements: indices 1,2,3 have 3,5,0 -> after shift, indices 2,3,4 get 3,5,0? Let's trace stepwise: To insert at pos1, we need to shift elements from index1 to m-1 (index2) right by one. Current arr1 indices: 0:1, 1:3, 2:5, 3:0, 4:0, 5:0. Shift: copy arr1[2]=5 to arr1[3], then arr1[1]=3 to arr1[2]. So arr1 becomes [1,3,3,5,0,0]. Then set arr1[1]=2: arr1=[1,2,3,5,0,0]. m becomes 4. Next arr2[1]=4: find pos: compare with arr1[0]=1, arr1[1]=2, arr1[2]=3, arr1[3]=5 -> insert at pos3. Shift arr1[3:4] right: arr1[3]=5 to arr1[4]: arr1=[1,2,3,5,5,0]. Set arr1[3]=4: arr1=[1,2,3,4,5,0]. m=5. Next arr2[2]=6: find pos: compare with 1,2,3,4,5 -> insert at pos5. Shift arr1[5:] right (no elements to shift as pos5 is at end). Set arr1[5]=6: arr1=[1,2,3,4,5,6]. Done.

Start: arr1=[2,4,0,0], arr2=[1,3], m=2, n=2. Process arr2[0]=1: find pos in arr1: compare with 2 (pos0) -> insert at pos0. Shift arr1[0:2] right: arr1[1]=4 to arr1[2], arr1[0]=2 to arr1[1]: arr1 becomes [2,2,4,0]. Set arr1[0]=1: arr1=[1,2,4,0]. m=3. Process arr2[1]=3: find pos: compare with 1,2,4 -> insert at pos2. Shift arr1[2:3] right: arr1[2]=4 to arr1[3]: arr1=[1,2,4,4]. Set arr1[2]=3: arr1=[1,2,3,4]. m=4. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Array with Shifting
ladders.desc: Insert a single element into a sorted array by shifting elements to make space.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation in the in-place merge approach. The intuition is to maintain the sorted order of an array when inserting a new element. Given a sorted array with some empty slots (or extra capacity), we find the correct position for the new element by comparing it with existing elements from the start. Once the position is found, we shift all elements from that position to the end of the sorted portion to the right by one index to create a gap, then place the new element in that gap. This ensures the array remains sorted. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements in the sorted portion, as we might need to shift all elements in the worst case (e.g., inserting at the beginning). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use temporary variables for the element to insert and indices. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in L0 for each element in the second array. In L0, we iterate through arr2 and for each element, use this subroutine to insert it into arr1. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in shifting (e.g., shifting from the wrong index or overwriting elements), not updating the size of the sorted portion after insertion, and inefficient shifting by moving elements one by one in a loop instead of using a single loop from the end. Real code pattern: Function insert_sorted(arr, size, element): find position pos such that arr[pos] is the first element greater than element (or size if all are smaller); for i from size-1 down to pos: arr[i+1] = arr[i]; arr[pos] = element; return size+1.

examples.trace: Start: arr=[1,3,5,0,0], size=3, element=2. Find pos: compare 2 with arr[0]=1 -> greater, arr[1]=3 -> less, so pos=1. Shift elements from index size-1=2 down to pos=1: i=2: arr[3]=arr[2]=5, arr becomes [1,3,5,5,0]; i=1: arr[2]=arr[1]=3, arr becomes [1,3,3,5,0]. Set arr[pos]=arr[1]=2: arr=[1,2,3,5,0]. New size=4.

Start: arr=[2,4,0,0], size=2, element=1. Find pos: compare 1 with arr[0]=2 -> less, so pos=0. Shift elements from index size-1=1 down to pos=0: i=1: arr[2]=arr[1]=4, arr becomes [2,4,4,0]; i=0: arr[1]=arr[0]=2, arr becomes [2,2,4,0]. Set arr[pos]=arr[0]=1: arr=[1,2,4,0]. New size=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Insertion Position in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Determine where to insert an element in a sorted array to maintain order.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight for the in-place merge: identifying the correct index to insert a new element into a sorted array without disrupting the order. The intuition is that in a sorted array, elements are in non-decreasing order, so to insert a new element, we need to find the first position where the existing element is greater than or equal to the new element. This ensures that after insertion, all elements before are less than or equal, and all after are greater than or equal, preserving sortedness. We do this by linear search: start from the beginning and compare the new element with each element until we find one that is larger or reach the end. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the size of the sorted portion, as we may need to check all elements in the worst case (e.g., inserting at the end). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is the first step in the insert_sorted subroutine; after finding the position, L1 handles the shifting and insertion. In L1, we use this to get 'pos', then shift elements from pos onward. Common mistakes include using binary search (which is O(log k) but doesn't reduce overall O(m*n) since shifting dominates), incorrect comparison for duplicate elements (should use <= or >= based on stability requirements), and not handling the case when the new element is larger than all existing elements (position should be at the end). Real code pattern: Function find_position(arr, size, element): for i in range(size): if arr[i] > element: return i; return size.

examples.trace: Start: arr=[1,3,5], size=3, element=2. Compare with arr[0]=1: 2 > 1, continue. Compare with arr[1]=3: 2 < 3, so position=1. Done.
Start: arr=[2,4], size=2, element=1. Compare with arr[0]=2: 1 < 2, so position=0. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Shifting Array Elements Right by One Position
ladders.desc: Move a range of elements in an array one index to the right to create space.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for making room to insert a new element in an array. The intuition is that arrays have fixed indices, so to insert at a specific position, we need to shift all elements from that position to the end to the right by one, which overwrites the next index but preserves order. We do this by iterating from the last element of the range backward to the start, copying each element to index+1. This backward iteration prevents overwriting elements before they are copied. Time complexity is O(s) where s is the number of elements shifted, as we move each element once. Space complexity is O(1) if done in-place with a temporary variable. Connection to L2: After finding the insertion position in L2, this shifting operation creates the gap for insertion in L1. In L1, we shift elements from 'pos' to 'size-1' right by one. Common mistakes include shifting forward (which overwrites elements), shifting the wrong range (e.g., from start instead of end), and not using a temporary variable when shifting in a loop (though it's fine to assign directly if done backward). Real code pattern: Function shift_right(arr, start, end): for i from end down to start: arr[i+1] = arr[i]; ensure arr has enough capacity.

examples.trace: Start: arr=[1,3,5,0,0], shift elements from index start=1 to end=2 right by one. Iterate backward: i=2: copy arr[2]=5 to arr[3], arr becomes [1,3,5,5,0]; i=1: copy arr[1]=3 to arr[2], arr becomes [1,3,3,5,0]. Done.

Start: arr=[2,4,0,0], shift elements from index start=0 to end=1 right by one. Iterate backward: i=1: copy arr[1]=4 to arr[2], arr becomes [2,4,4,0]; i=0: copy arr[0]=2 to arr[1], arr becomes [2,2,4,0]. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Compare two elements to determine their order (less than, equal, greater than).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used throughout the merge process to decide where to insert elements. The intuition is that sorting relies on comparisons: to merge sorted arrays, we need to compare elements from both arrays to place them in correct order. In the in-place approach, we compare the element to insert with elements in the sorted array to find its position. This is a simple value comparison using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=). Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used in L2 to find the insertion position; in L2, we compare the new element with each element in the array until we find the right spot. In L3, shifting doesn't involve comparison directly, but it follows from the comparison result. Common mistakes include using incorrect operators (e.g., < instead of <= for stable sort), comparing indices instead of values, and not handling edge cases like empty arrays or equal elements. Real code pattern: In a loop: if arr[i] > element: then position found; else continue.

examples.trace: Element to insert is 2, current array element is 1. Since 2 > 1, the insertion position is not here; we need to check next element.
Element to insert is 1, current array element is 2. Since 1 < 2, this is the first element greater than 1, so insertion position is at index 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Basic array operations: read and write elements at specific positions.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for working with arrays in the merge algorithm. The intuition is that arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be accessed or modified using its index (position). In the in-place merge, we constantly read elements to compare them and write elements to shift or insert new values. For example, arr[i] reads the element at index i, and arr[i] = value writes a new value to that index. Time complexity for access or modification is O(1) since arrays support random access. Space complexity is O(1) per operation. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the comparisons in L4 (e.g., reading arr[i] to compare with an element) and the shifting in L3 (e.g., writing arr[i+1] = arr[i]). In L0, we use these operations throughout the algorithm. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., negative or beyond array length), confusing zero-based indexing (first element at index 0), and not initializing arrays properly. Real code pattern: To read: value = arr[index]; to write: arr[index] = value; ensure index is within 0 to len(arr)-1.

examples.input: arr=[1,3,5], read index=1, write index=2 with value=10
examples.trace: Start with arr=[1,3,5]. Read arr[1]: returns 3. Then write arr[2]=10: arr becomes [1,3,10].

examples.input: arr=[2,4,0], read index=0, write index=1 with value=6
examples.trace: Start with arr=[2,4,0]. Read arr[0]: returns 2. Then write arr[1]=6: arr becomes [2,6,0].
